skill.name: bdi-mental-states
skill.description: Model Belief-Desire-Intention mental states from RDF context using ontology patterns, SPARQL competency checks, and framework integration guidance.
skill.summary: Convert RDF context into structured BDI beliefs, desires, intentions, plans, and explainable reasoning chains.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, crypto, C2, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are mostly Markdown ontology examples, SPARQL snippets, and BDI terminology rather than executable malicious code. The skill has no installer, package manifest, or runtime script, but reference code includes networked triple-store examples and string-formatted SPARQL queries that need safety warnings if copied into production.

medium_findings.title: Reference Code Opens Caller-Supplied SPARQL Endpoints
medium_findings.description: A documentation example constructs a SPARQLUpdateStore from an endpoint parameter and opens query and update URLs. This is not executable by the skill itself, but users who copy it should restrict endpoints and avoid sending sensitive mental-state data to untrusted stores.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network pattern is real in the sample code, but the repository contains documentation only and no runtime entrypoint. Risk depends on whether users copy the example into an agent system.
medium_findings.remediation: Add guidance to validate endpoint allowlists, use TLS, authenticate stores, and avoid exposing private agent state to untrusted SPARQL services.

medium_findings.title: SPARQL Query Examples Use String Interpolation
medium_findings.description: Reference code builds SPARQL queries with percent formatting for URI and timestamp values. If copied with untrusted inputs, this pattern can lead to SPARQL injection or malformed queries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible and the variables represent externally meaningful identifiers. The confidence is moderate because the code is illustrative documentation, not an installed executable component.
medium_findings.remediation: Use RDFLib URIRef validation, parameterized query builders where available, strict URI schemes, and escaping before running queries against a store.

low_findings.title: Static Command and Crypto Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged backticks, ontology prefixes, hashes, C2-looking query text, and words such as desire, intention, execute, and commit inside Markdown examples. Review found no shell execution, weak cryptographic implementation, C2 behavior, or reconnaissance code in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are inside Markdown code fences, tables, or ontology examples. No executable shell, cryptographic API, malware command channel, or host reconnaissance behavior is present.
low_findings.remediation: No security fix is required for these static hits. Consider scanner tuning for Markdown ontology examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Triple Store Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample triple-store class opens remote query and update endpoints. This is a legitimate integration pattern, but it should be implemented with endpoint controls and data handling safeguards.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly opens network endpoints. It is not invoked by the skill, so the risk is limited to downstream implementation.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Document trusted endpoint configuration and require authentication for writable SPARQL stores.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interpolated SPARQL Query Strings
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples interpolate values directly into SPARQL strings. Downstream implementations should validate URIs and literals before query execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe construction pattern is explicit, but it appears only in reference code. Exploitability requires adoption with untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Validate URI and datetime inputs before interpolation, or use safer RDF query construction APIs.

content.value_statement: Teams building cognitive agents need a consistent way to represent beliefs, desires, intentions, plans, and evidence. This skill provides ontology patterns, SPARQL checks, and integration guidance for BDI mental-state modeling.
actual_capabilities: Maps RDF world-state context into beliefs, desires, intentions, goals, plans, and tasks.
Defines ontology relationships for motivation, fulfillment, support, generation, and temporal validity.
Provides SPARQL competency queries for validating mental-state graphs.
Shows RDF examples for workflows, multi-agent coordination, conflict resolution, and payment scenarios.
Explains integration patterns for SEMAS, JADE, JADEX, FIPA ACL, triple stores, and LAG pipelines.
Supports explainability through explicit justification links and traceable cognitive chains.
limitations: It is documentation and examples, not a runnable BDI engine.
It does not include tests, package metadata, or production deployment scripts.
Example code requires validation, authentication, and endpoint controls before production use.
The ontology patterns may need adaptation for domain-specific agent frameworks.

use_cases.title: Design Cognitive Agent State Models
use_cases.description: Create a formal mental-state graph that separates beliefs, desires, intentions, plans, and world states.

use_cases.title: Validate Semantic Agent Data
use_cases.description: Use SPARQL competency questions to check whether BDI graphs satisfy modeling expectations.

use_cases.title: Bridge Ontologies and Agent Frameworks
use_cases.description: Translate BDI ontology concepts into SEMAS rules, JADE structures, JADEX goals, or FIPA messages.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Mental States
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner mapping from a short scenario.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bdi-mental-states skill. Given this scenario, list the likely beliefs, desires, intentions, world states, and justifications.

prompt_templates.title: Create RDF BDI Triples
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert context into a semantic graph.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bdi-mental-states skill. Transform this RDF context into BDI Turtle triples with beliefs, desires, intentions, plans, and validity intervals.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a BDI Graph
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bdi-mental-states skill. Review this BDI graph against competency questions and identify missing motivation, support, plan, or temporal links.

prompt_templates.title: Design Framework Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bdi-mental-states skill. Design a SEMAS, JADE, or triple-store integration for this agent workflow, including data validation and security controls.

output_examples.input: A commuter sees heavy traffic and wants to arrive at work by 9 AM.
output_examples.output: The skill would describe a traffic world state, a delay belief, an on-time arrival desire, an alternate-route intention, and a plan with ordered commute tasks.

output_examples.input: Two agents share a deadline but have different responsibilities.
output_examples.output: The skill would model a shared deadline belief, role-specific desires, committed intentions, and communication links for coordination.

output_examples.input: A BDI graph lacks support links for intentions.
output_examples.output: The skill would flag missing supporting beliefs and recommend competency queries to verify motivation, fulfillment, and justification chains.

best_practices: Model world states separately from agent beliefs so evidence and perspective remain clear.
Attach validity intervals and justifications to mental states for traceable reasoning.
Validate generated RDF with competency queries before using it in planning or execution.
anti_patterns: Do not treat desires or intentions as direct actions without an explicit plan layer.
Do not omit temporal bounds when mental states can change over time.
Do not copy networked triple-store examples into production without endpoint and input validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help model?
faq.answer: It helps model agent beliefs, desires, intentions, goals, plans, tasks, world states, and justifications using RDF and ontology patterns.

faq.question: Does it run a BDI agent?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation, examples, and patterns for designing or validating a BDI agent model.

faq.question: Can it generate Turtle RDF?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Turtle examples and guidance for representing BDI mental states as RDF triples.

faq.question: Can it validate an ontology graph?
faq.answer: It provides SPARQL competency queries that can reveal missing links, invalid structures, and incomplete reasoning chains.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Treat example endpoints, model output, RDF input, and SPARQL strings as untrusted until validated in your implementation.
